commit 2df4a9a8beb4dbf6613ede86cae5915731f6a5eb
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Swap

This commit fills in the swap.rst stub as part of the structured memory
management documentation following Mel Gorman's book outline.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/swap.rst b/Documentation/mm/swap.rst
> index 78819bd4d745..89a93cc081d4 100644
> --- a/Documentation/mm/swap.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/swap.rst
> @@ -3,3 +3,157 @@
[ ... ]
> +Cluster Allocation
> +------------------
> +
> +Swap space is allocated in clusters (groups of contiguous slots, typically
> +32 pages).  Each cluster tracks which slots are free and whether it has

Is the cluster size typically 32 pages?

The macro for swap disk allocation clusters, SWAPFILE_CLUSTER, is usually 256
(or HPAGE_PMD_NR, typically 512). The value 32 appears to correspond to
SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX, which is a distinct concept used as a batch size limit for
page reclaim.

> +pending discards.  Per-CPU hints point to the most recently used cluster
> +so that allocations from the same CPU tend to land in the same cluster,
[ ... ]
> +Swap Cache
> +==========
> +
> +The swap cache keeps recently swapped-in (or about to be swapped-out)
> +pages in memory, indexed by their swap entry.  This serves several
> +purposes:
> +
> +- **Deduplication**: when multiple processes share a swapped page (via
> +  ``fork()``), only one copy is read from disk; subsequent faults find
> +  the page in the swap cache.
> +- **Write coalescing**: if a page is modified and swapped out again before
> +  the previous write completes, the swap cache absorbs the update without
> +  issuing a new write.

Does the swap cache actually absorb the update without issuing a new write?

If a page is modified while a previous write is in-flight (PG_writeback is
set), the old data is already being written to disk. The page becomes dirty
again (PG_dirty), so the kernel must issue a new write operation to persist
the new modifications after the current writeback completes.

> +- **Readahead**: when one page is swapped in, adjacent swap entries are
> +  speculatively read to exploit spatial and temporal locality.
[ ... ]
> +Writeback
> +---------
> +
> +zswap writeback decompresses the page, allocates a swap slot, and writes
> +the uncompressed page to the swap device.  This is the slow path —

Does zswap writeback allocate a new swap slot?

The swap slot (swp_entry_t) is already allocated by the core swap code
(folio_alloc_swap()) before the page is ever passed to zswap_store().
During writeback, zswap_writeback_entry() uses this pre-existing swap entry
to allocate a swap cache folio and write to the already-allocated disk slot.

> +ideally most pages are either faulted back in from the compressed cache
> +or freed without ever reaching disk.
[ ... ]
> +Swap I/O
> +========
> +
> +``mm/page_io.c`` handles the mechanics of reading and writing pages to
> +swap.  The I/O path checks three layers in order before falling through to
> +disk:
> +
> +1. The zero page bitmap — if the slot is known to be zero-filled, return
> +   a zeroed page (read) or set the bit (write) with no I/O.
> +2. zswap — if enabled, attempt to store/load the page in the compressed
> +   cache.
> +3. Block I/O — submit a bio to the swap device, using the swap extent
> +   tree to map the slot to a disk sector.
> +
> +For swap files (as opposed to raw partitions), the I/O follows the
> +filesystem's block mapping rather than issuing direct device I/O.

Do swap files avoid direct device I/O?

Standard swap I/O operations (like swap_writepage_bdev_async) use the
swap_extent tree built during swapon to submit raw bios directly to the
underlying block device. This bypasses the filesystem's normal I/O routines.
Only specialized filesystems (like NFS with SWP_FS_OPS set) fall back to
the filesystem's a_ops->swap_rw.

Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>
Date:   Sat Mar 14 15:25:36 2026 +0000

    Docs/mm: document Swap
    
    Fill in the swap.rst stub created in commit 481cc97349d6
    ("mm,doc: Add new documentation structure") as part of
    the structured memory management documentation following
    Mel Gorman's book outline.
    
    Signed-off-by: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

diff --git a/Documentation/mm/swap.rst b/Documentation/mm/swap.rst
index 78819bd4d745..89a93cc081d4 100644
--- a/Documentation/mm/swap.rst
+++ b/Documentation/mm/swap.rst
@@ -3,3 +3,157 @@
 ====
 Swap
 ====
+
+Swap allows the kernel to evict anonymous pages (those not backed by a
+file) to a swap device so that physical memory can be reused.  When the
+pages are needed again, they are read back in.  The swap subsystem spans
+several files: ``mm/swapfile.c`` manages swap devices, ``mm/swap_state.c``
+implements the swap cache, ``mm/page_io.c`` handles disk I/O, and
+``mm/zswap.c`` provides an optional compressed cache layer.
+
+.. contents:: :local:
+
+Swap Entries
+============
+
+A swap entry is a compact identifier that encodes which swap device to use
+and the offset within that device.  When a page is swapped out, its page
+table entry is replaced with a swap entry so that the kernel knows where to
+find the data on a subsequent fault.  Swap entries are also used internally
+as keys into the swap cache.
+
+Swap Devices
+============
+
+A swap device is a disk partition or file registered with the ``swapon()``
+system call.  Each device is described by a ``swap_info_struct`` that holds
+the device's extent map, cluster state, and per-CPU allocation hints.
+
+The kernel maps virtual swap offsets to disk locations through a tree of
+``swap_extent`` structures.  For raw partitions the mapping is trivial
+(one extent covering the whole device); for swap files the mapping follows
+the file's block layout on disk.
+
+Cluster Allocation
+------------------
+
+Swap space is allocated in clusters (groups of contiguous slots, typically
+32 pages).  Each cluster tracks which slots are free and whether it has
+pending discards.  Per-CPU hints point to the most recently used cluster
+so that allocations from the same CPU tend to land in the same cluster,
+improving spatial locality for both SSDs and spinning disks.
+
+When a cluster is full, the allocator scans for a new one.  Under heavy
+swap pressure, it may also reclaim slots from full clusters if the pages
+they reference have since been freed or swapped back in.
+
+TRIM / Discard
+--------------
+
+For SSD-backed swap, the kernel can issue discard (TRIM) commands when
+swap slots are freed.  This is batched per-cluster: once all slots in a
+cluster are free, a single discard is issued for the entire range.  This
+avoids the overhead of per-page discards while still informing the device
+that the blocks are unused.
+
+Swap counts
+-----------
+
+Each swap slot has a reference count tracking how many page table entries
+point to it (due to ``fork()`` and copy-on-write).  For slots referenced
+by very many processes, a continuation mechanism extends the counter
+beyond its inline capacity.
+
+Swap Cache
+==========
+
+The swap cache keeps recently swapped-in (or about to be swapped-out)
+pages in memory, indexed by their swap entry.  This serves several
+purposes:
+
+- **Deduplication**: when multiple processes share a swapped page (via
+  ``fork()``), only one copy is read from disk; subsequent faults find
+  the page in the swap cache.
+- **Write coalescing**: if a page is modified and swapped out again before
+  the previous write completes, the swap cache absorbs the update without
+  issuing a new write.
+- **Readahead**: when one page is swapped in, adjacent swap entries are
+  speculatively read to exploit spatial and temporal locality.
+
+The swap cache is implemented as a per-cluster array of pointers
+(the "swap table"), providing O(1) lookup by swap entry.
+See also Documentation/mm/swap-table.rst.
+
+Readahead
+---------
+
+Swap readahead pre-fetches pages from swap before they are faulted in.
+Two strategies are used:
+
+- **Cluster readahead**: reads a window of swap entries around the faulting
+  entry, betting on spatial locality in the swap device.
+- **VMA readahead**: uses the virtual address layout to predict which swap
+  entries will be needed next, which is more effective when the access
+  pattern follows the process's address space layout rather than the swap
+  device layout.
+
+``vm.page-cluster`` controls the readahead window size (as a power of two).
+
+Compressed Swap (zswap)
+=======================
+
+zswap (``mm/zswap.c``) is an optional write-behind compressed cache that
+sits between the reclaim path and the swap device.  When reclaim evicts a
+page, zswap attempts to compress it and store the compressed data in a
+RAM-based pool (using the zsmalloc allocator).
+
+If the page is faulted back in before the pool fills, no disk I/O occurs —
+the page is decompressed directly from memory.  This is significantly
+faster than reading from even an SSD.
+
+Pool Management
+---------------
+
+Each zswap pool pairs a compression algorithm (lzo, lz4, zstd, etc.) with
+a zsmalloc memory pool.  Per-CPU compression contexts avoid lock
+contention during compression and decompression.
+
+When the pool reaches its size limit (controlled by
+``/sys/module/zswap/parameters/max_pool_percent``), the oldest entries are
+evicted: zswap writes them out to the backing swap device, falling back to
+the normal swap I/O path.  An LRU list tracks entries for this purpose.
+
+Writeback
+---------
+
+zswap writeback decompresses the page, allocates a swap slot, and writes
+the uncompressed page to the swap device.  This is the slow path —
+ideally most pages are either faulted back in from the compressed cache
+or freed without ever reaching disk.
+
+Zero-Filled Pages
+=================
+
+``mm/page_io.c`` maintains a bitmap (``swap_zeromap``) tracking swap slots
+that contained zero-filled pages.  When such a page is swapped in, the
+kernel returns a zeroed page without performing any I/O.  When a zero
+page is swapped out, the bitmap bit is set instead of issuing a write.
+This optimization is significant for workloads that allocate large amounts
+of memory that is never written to.
+
+Swap I/O
+========
+
+``mm/page_io.c`` handles the mechanics of reading and writing pages to
+swap.  The I/O path checks three layers in order before falling through to
+disk:
+
+1. The zero page bitmap — if the slot is known to be zero-filled, return
+   a zeroed page (read) or set the bit (write) with no I/O.
+2. zswap — if enabled, attempt to store/load the page in the compressed
+   cache.
+3. Block I/O — submit a bio to the swap device, using the swap extent
+   tree to map the slot to a disk sector.
+
+For swap files (as opposed to raw partitions), the I/O follows the
+filesystem's block mapping rather than issuing direct device I/O.

    "content": "{"content":"diff --git a/Documentation/mm/swap.rst b/Documentation/mm/swap.rst\nindex 78819bd4d745..89a93cc081d4 100644\n--- a/Documentation/mm/swap.rst\n+++ b/Documentation/mm/swap.rst\n@@ -3,3 +3,157 @@\n ====\n Swap\n ====\n+\n+Swap allows the kernel to evict anonymous pages (those not backed by a\n+file) to a swap device so that physical memory can be reused.  When the\n+pages are needed again, they are read back in.  The swap subsystem spans\n+several files: ``mm/swapfile.c`` manages swap devices, ``mm/swap_state.c``\n+implements the swap cache, ``mm/page_io.c`` handles disk I/O, and\n+``mm/zswap.c`` provides an optional compressed cache layer.\n+\n+.. contents:: :local:\n+\n+Swap Entries\n+============\n+\n+A swap entry is a compact identifier that encodes which swap device to use\n+and the offset within that device.  When a page is swapped out, its page\n+table entry is replaced with a swap entry so that the kernel knows where to\n+find the data on a subsequent fault.  Swap entries are also used internally\n+as keys into the swap cache.\n+\n+Swap Devices\n+============\n+\n+A swap device is a disk partition or file registered with the ``swapon()``\n+system call.  Each device is described by a ``swap_info_struct`` that holds\n+the device's extent map, cluster state, and per-CPU allocation hints.\n+\n+The kernel maps virtual swap offsets to disk locations through a tree of\n+``swap_extent`` structures.  For raw partitions the mapping is trivial\n+(one extent covering the whole device); for swap files the mapping follows\n+the file's block layout on disk.\n+\n+Cluster Allocation\n+------------------\n+\n+Swap space is allocated in clusters (groups of contiguous slots, typically\n+32 pages).  Each cluster tracks which slots are free and whether it has\n+pending discards.  Per-CPU hints point to the most recently used cluster\n+so that allocations from the same CPU tend to land in the same cluster,\n+improving spatial locality for both SSDs and spinning disks.\n+\n+When a cluster is full, the allocator scans for a new one.  Under heavy\n+swap pressure, it may also reclaim slots from full clusters if the pages\n+they reference have since been freed or swapped back in.\n+\n+TRIM / Discard\n+--------------\n+\n+For SSD-backed swap, the kernel can issue discard (TRIM) commands when\n+swap slots are freed.  This is batched per-cluster: once all slots in a\n+cluster are free, a single discard is issued for the entire range.  This\n+avoids the overhead of per-page discards while still informing the device\n+that the blocks are unused.\n+\n+Swap counts\n+-----------\n+\n+Each swap slot has a reference count tracking how many page table entries\n+point to it (due to ``fork()`` and copy-on-write).  For slots referenced\n+by very many processes, a continuation mechanism extends the counter\n+beyond its inline capacity.\n+\n+Swap Cache\n+==========\n+\n+The swap cache keeps recently swapped-in (or about to be swapped-out)\n+pages in memory, indexed by their swap entry.  This serves several\n+purposes:\n+\n+- **Deduplication**: when multiple processes share a swapped page (via\n+  ``fork()``), only one copy is read from disk; subsequent faults find\n+  the page in the swap cache.\n+- **Write coalescing**: if a page is modified and swapped out again before\n+  the previous write completes, the swap cache absorbs the update without\n+  issuing a new write.\n+- **Readahead**: when one page is swapped in, adjacent swap entries are\n+  speculatively read to exploit spatial and temporal locality.\n+\n+The swap cache is implemented as a per-cluster array of pointers\n+(the \"swap table\"), providing O(1) lookup by swap entry.\n+See also Documentation/mm/swap-table.rst.\n+\n+Readahead\n+---------\n+\n+Swap readahead pre-fetches pages from swap before they are faulted in.\n+Two strategies are used:\n+\n+- **Cluster readahead**: reads a window of swap entries around the faulting\n+  entry, betting on spatial locality in the swap device.\n+- **VMA readahead**: uses the virtual address layout to predict which swap\n+  entries will be needed next, which is more effective when the access\n+  pattern follows the process's address space layout rather than the swap\n+  device layout.\n+\n+``vm.page-cluster`` controls the readahead window size (as a power of two).\n+\n+Compressed Swap (zswap)\n+=======================\n+\n+zswap (``mm/zswap.c``) is an optional write-behind compressed cache that\n+sits between the reclaim path and the swap device.  When reclaim evicts a\n+page, zswap attempts to compress it and store the compressed data in a\n+RAM-based pool (using the zsmalloc allocator).\n+\n+If the page is faulted back in before the pool fills, no disk I/O occurs —\n+the page is decompressed directly from memory.  This is significantly\n+faster than reading from even an SSD.\n+\n+Pool Management\n+---------------\n+\n+Each zswap pool pairs a compression algorithm (lzo, lz4, zstd, etc.) with\n+a zsmalloc memory pool.  Per-CPU compression contexts avoid lock\n+contention during compression and decompression.\n+\n+When the pool reaches its size limit (controlled by\n+``/sys/module/zswap/parameters/max_pool_percent``), the oldest entries are\n+evicted: zswap writes them out to the backing swap device, falling back to\n+the normal swap I/O path.  An LRU list tracks entries for this purpose.\n+\n+Writeback\n+---------\n+\n+zswap writeback decompresses the page, allocates a swap slot, and writes\n+the uncompressed page to the swap device.  This is the slow path —\n+ideally most pages are either faulted back in from the compressed cache\n+or freed without ever reaching disk.\n+\n+Zero-Filled Pages\n+=================\n+\n+``mm/page_io.c`` maintains a bitmap (``swap_zeromap``) tracking swap slots\n+that contained zero-filled pages.  When such a page is swapped in, the\n+kernel returns a zeroed page without performing any I/O.  When a zero\n+page is swapped out, the bitmap bit is set instead of issuing a write.\n+This optimization is significant for workloads that allocate large amounts\n+of memory that is never written to.\n+\n+Swap I/O\n+========\n+\n+``mm/page_io.c`` handles the mechanics of reading and writing pages to\n+swap.     "thought": "**My Analysis of Swap Read Folio Implementation**

Okay, let's break down this `swap_read_folio` function. My initial impression, looking at the call flow, is that it tries a few different methods to read a folio from swap: first, it checks the zero page map with `swap_read_folio_zeromap(folio)`. If that fails, it tries zswap with `zswap_load(folio)`. Finally, if both of those fail, it falls back to either `swap_read_folio_fs`, `swap_read_folio_bdev_sync`, or `swap_read_folio_bdev_async`. The order is important:

1.  Zero map
2.  zswap
3.  Block I/O

That lines up perfectly with the stated overview.

Now, that "swap files follow the filesystem's block mapping" line... that needs a closer look. If `SWP_FS_OPS` is set, `swap_read_folio_fs` *is* called. This allocates a `swap_iocb` and then calls `mapping->a_ops->swap_rw()`. However, `swap_rw` is only implemented by network and specialized filesystems like `nfs`, `cifs`, and `smb`.

For local filesystems like ext4, btrfs, and xfs, `SWP_FS_OPS` is *not* set. So, in those cases, it calls `swap_read_folio_bdev_async` (or the sync version). This builds a `bio` with `bio->bi_iter.bi_sector` set using the swap extent tree, and `swap_folio_sector(folio)`. This is the extent map I set up at `swapon` time. So, contrary to the text, I'm pretty sure it's issuing direct device I/O using the extent map. The statement is incorrect. It only applies to the network and special filesystems.
I'll verify this for my own sanity.

Block I/O — submit a bio to the swap device, using the swap extent\n   tree to map the slot to a disk sector.\n\nFor swap files (as opposed to raw partitions), the I/O follows the\nfilesystem's block mapping rather than issuing direct device I/O.","end_line":159,"start_line":153,"total_lines":159}",

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**My Understanding of Swap File I/O Discrepancies**

Okay, hold on a minute. The text seems to have a serious contradiction here. It first states that for swap files, the I/O *follows* the filesystem's block mapping, not direct device I/O, which is contrary to previous points, but later states that bios are issued directly to the block device. It's almost as if the author missed the mark. Let me clarify this in my mind: For raw partitions, the extent tree handles the mapping directly, which is straightforward. This works just fine for *both* swap partitions and local swap files. However, for swap files, when the kernel is setting up swapon, it asks the filesystem for the disk extents via something like `bmap`. It *builds* the extent tree, and then issues those direct I/O (bios) directly to the block device. It *bypasses* the normal filesystem I/O path entirely. The text's initial assertion about following the filesystem's block mapping is simply incorrect, a significant misunderstanding.

The statement about swap allowing eviction of anonymous pages to swap devices is mostly correct, and that makes sense in the context of memory management.

Hmm, "swap table".... that's a new one. I think I know that commit. Let me just confirm that the swap table was recently added. Let me check commit `f83938e4188c`. Yes, confirmed.

    "content": "{"content":"commit 8578e0c00dcf0c58fbc32d4904ecaf8e802a6590\nAuthor: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>\nDate:   Wed Sep 17 00:00:56 2025 +0800\n\n    mm, swap: use the swap table for the swap cache and switch API\n    \n    Introduce basic swap table infrastructures, which are now just a\n    fixed-sized flat array inside each swap cluster, with access wrappers.\n    \n    Each cluster contains a swap table of 512 entries.  Each table entry is an\n    opaque atomic long.  It could be in 3 types: a shadow type (XA_VALUE), a\n    folio type (pointer), or NULL.\n    \n    In this first step, it only supports storing a folio or shadow, and it is\n    a drop-in replacement for the current swap cache.  Convert all swap cache\n    users to use the new sets of APIs.  Chris Li has been suggesting using a\n    new infrastructure for swap cache for better performance, and that idea\n    combined well with the swap table as the new backing structure.  Now the\n    lock contention range is reduced to 2M clusters, which is much smaller\n    than the 64M address_space.  And we can also drop the multiple\n    address_space design.\n    \n    All the internal works are done with swap_cache_get_* helpers.  Swap cache\n    lookup is still lock-less like before, and the helper's contexts are same\n    with original swap cache helpers.  They still require a pin on the swap\n    device to prevent the backing data from being freed.\n    \n    Swap cache updates are now protected by the swap cluster lock instead of\n    the XArray lock.  This is mostly handled internally, but new\n    __swap_cache_* helpers require the caller to lock the cluster.  So, a few\n    new cluster access and locking helpers are also introduced.\n    \n    A fully cluster-based unified swap table can be implemented on top of this\n    to take care of all count tracking and synchronization work, with dynamic\n    allocation.  It should reduce the memory usage while making the\n    performance even better.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20250916160100.31545-12-ryncsn@gmail.com\n    Co-developed-by: Chris Li <chrisl@kernel.org>\n    Signed-off-by: Chris Li <chrisl@kernel.org>\n    Signed-off-by: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>\n    Acked-by: Chris Li <chrisl@kernel.org>\n    Suggested-by: Chris Li <chrisl@kernel.org>\n    Cc: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>\n    Cc: Baoquan He <bhe@redhat.com>\n    Cc: Barry Song <baohua@kernel.org>\n    Cc: David Hildenbrand <david@redhat.com>\n    Cc: \"Huang, Ying\" <ying.huang@linux.alibaba.com>\n    Cc: Hugh Dickins <hughd@google.com>\n    Cc: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\n    Cc: Kemeng Shi <shikemeng@huaweicloud.com>\n    Cc: kernel test robot <oliver.sang@intel.com>\n    Cc: Lorenzo Stoakes <lorenzo.stoakes@oracle.com>\n    Cc: Matthew Wilcox (Oracle) <willy@infradead.org>\n    Cc: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>\n    Cc: Yosry Ahmed <yosryahmed@google.com>\n    Cc: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>\n    Cc: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>","end_line":59,"start_line":1,"total_lines":59}",

    "thought": "**My Assessment of the Documentation**

Okay, so the documentation *does* accurately describe the swap cache as an array of pointers within each swap cluster – that's a pleasant surprise! The commit is dated September 17, 2025, so the information should be current. But, hold on a second... the prompt wants me to verify that the code changes *actually* implement what the commit message claims. Right.

The commit message says it's filling in a swap documentation stub as part of structured memory management docs, following Mel Gorman's book.

I immediately spot a significant issue: the documentation states that "For swap files (as opposed to raw partitions), the I/O follows the filesystem's block mapping rather than issuing direct device I/O." This is categorically *incorrect* for almost all swap files. The `swapon` command utilizes the `bmap` interface to determine the file's disk extents and creates a `swap_extent` tree. Subsequently, `mm/page_io.c` bypasses the filesystem altogether and issues direct I/O (using `bio`) to the underlying block device, unless it's a network filesystem flagged with `SWP_FS_OPS`. I'm going to categorize this as a factual inaccuracy, a misrepresentation of the kernel's swap architecture, not just a "missing piece."

Moving on, I'm examining this passage:
```
+zswap writeback decompresses the page, allocates a swap slot, and writes
+the uncompressed page to the swap device.  This is the slow path —
+ideally most pages are either faulted back in from the compressed cache
+or freed without ever reaching disk.
```

No, this is wrong. Zswap writeback *doesn't* allocate a swap slot! The swap slot was already allocated when the page was initially evicted to swap and intercepted by zswap. Zswap merely decompresses the page and writes it to the *already allocated* swap slot. Wait, let me just double-check my assumptions.

Okay, does zswap allocate a swap slot *during* writeback? In older kernels, yes, the swap slot was allocated before zswap. But wasn't there a recent patch series, "mm: zswap: delayed swap slot allocation"? I don't think so. Zswap is, fundamentally, a swap cache; it intercepts the page before it goes to the swap device. A swap entry is already allocated by `add_to_swap` (or potentially `get_swap_device` and `get_swap_page`). Zswap then intercepts the `frontswap_store` or `zswap_store` operation. At that point, the `folio->swap` field (the swap slot) is already set. Zswap stores the compressed data, using the swap entry as a key. When writeback happens, zswap decompresses the data and writes it to the *existing* swap slot. It does *not* allocate a new one. I should probably go and verify that. Let me look at the code for zswap writeback...

    "thought": "**My Zswap Writeback Musings**

Okay, let me think this through. I'm looking at `zswap_writeback_entry` and it takes a `swp_entry_t swpentry`. The code then calls `swap_cache_alloc_folio` followed by `__swap_writepage(folio, NULL)`. The key question here is whether `zswap_writeback_entry` *allocates* a swap slot. No, that's wrong. The `swpentry` is already allocated; it's used to index the zswap cache using `entry->swpentry`. The swap slot *is* already reserved for this page!

So, the documentation in `swap.rst` is incorrect. It says that zswap writeback decompresses the page, allocates a swap slot, and writes the uncompressed page to the swap device. That is false. It decompresses the page and then writes it to the *already allocated* swap slot. It does *not* allocate a new slot during writeback. I need to make sure to update that documentation.

Now, let me shift gears and look at the zswap pool management. It says zswap pairs a compression algorithm with a zsmalloc memory pool. Per-CPU compression contexts, that's good for contention. But is it *strictly* zsmalloc? The text says zsmalloc. The zpool API is used, which defaults to zsmalloc. Could it be another zpool implementation? Technically, it could be zpool (zbud, z3fold, zsmalloc), but zsmalloc is the default and, practically, the only one really used these days. I think the other zpool allocators might have been removed. Let me just quickly confirm that... Yep, zbud and z3fold are indeed gone from the kernel recently! So the documentation is essentially correct, it's safe to say "zsmalloc".

    "thought": "**Analyzing Swap I/O and Write Coalescing**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was: the `__swap_writepage` function checks the `SWP_FS_OPS` flag.  This is only set for filesystems that use `swap_rw` but *don't* support direct block mapping (like NFS or CIFS).  For typical swap files on ext4 or xfs, `swapon` builds a `swap_extent` tree, and the I/O goes directly to the block device using `swap_writepage_bdev_async`, which uses a `bio` and issues it directly.

So, the documentation is misleading. It states, "For swap files (as opposed to raw partitions), the I/O follows the filesystem's block mapping *rather than* issuing direct device I/O."  That's incorrect. We *do* issue direct device I/O through `submit_bio`, after using the `swap_extent` tree to look up the mapping.  It's only if `SWP_FS_OPS` is set (NFS-like situations) that we fall back to the filesystem's `swap_rw` function. Concern number 5 is valid then.

Now, concerning the write coalescing issue, that's what's got me thinking. The documentation says, "if a page is modified and swapped out again before the previous write completes, the swap cache absorbs the update without issuing a new write." That's not right. If a page is under writeback (`PG_writeback`), it's *already* being written to disk. Any modifications while that write is in progress can't be absorbed, because the disk write may have already started or even finished, with the old data.  So, once the original write is done, a *new* write would be issued with the updated data.

Let's test this in the kernel: If a page is dirtied while under writeback, `folio_test_writeback(folio)` should be true. If the reclaimer tries to write it again, `swap_writepage` (or `add_to_swap`) should see it. The documentation implies the page is *already* under writeback.

If the page is in the swap cache but *not* under writeback, *then* it could absorb an update. So the "before the previous write completes" part implies the write has been issued.

The documentation seems to suggest:

*   T0: Swap out starts. Page is in swap cache, marked for writeback, and the write is issued.
*   T1: Page is modified (dirtied).
*   T2: "Swapped out again before the previous write completes".

The reclaim path would see the page under writeback, and either skip it or wait. It would *not* coalesce the write without a new write being issued. The page is dirty after that. Then a new write will need to be issued.

The phrase "before the previous write completes" could mean the page was swapped out, but the write was delayed. But if the write *hasn't* been issued yet, then there's no "previous write."  If a page is in the swap cache, it's a page cache page. If dirty, it will be written. Modified again? It's redirtied. If a write wasn't issued, maybe. But the phrase implies the write is *in progress*.

I'll check `mm/vmscan.c` or similar to see if `folio_test_writeback` prevents write coalescing. That's the key.

Okay, here's my assessment of the documentation patch for `Documentation/mm/swap.rst`. After thoroughly examining the code and the claims made in the patch, I've identified four key areas where the documentation needs correction or clarification. Since this is a documentation-only change, the severity assessment will focus on the potential for misunderstanding and the impact on developers reading the documentation, not on any direct runtime errors. I'll maintain a rigorous standard of checking against the source code to avoid false positives. All issues are confirmed.

1.  **Zswap Writeback Allocation:** The documentation currently misrepresents the `zswap` writeback process. It incorrectly suggests that a new swap slot is allocated during this stage. In reality, the swap slot (`swp_entry_t`) is *already* allocated by the core swap code, specifically during the `folio_alloc_swap()` call when a page is initially swapped out to disk. The `zswap_writeback_entry()` function then uses this *existing* swap entry to allocate a swap cache folio and to write the page data to the pre-allocated disk slot. This is a crucial distinction that the documentation should reflect, as it helps clarify the zswap interaction with the swap architecture. Therefore, the documentation should be updated to more accurately describe how the swap architecture works with zswap. This misunderstanding might hinder developers trying to follow the execution flow or to add new features that deal with zswap.

2.  **Swap Cluster Size:** The documentation refers to swap space allocation clusters as "typically 32 pages." This is misleading. While 32 might be relevant to other aspects, such as the reclaim batch size, the actual physical cluster size on the swap device is determined by the `SWAPFILE_CLUSTER` macro (which can be 256 or 512, based on the `HPAGE_PMD_NR` definition). This is a significant fact in swap file design. The documentation conflates two separate concepts: The physical allocation unit on the disk, and the algorithm for page reclamation. This is an important distinction and requires modification.

3.  **Direct I/O for Swap Files:** The documentation incorrectly states that swap files "do not issue direct device I/O".  While it's true that swap file I/O uses the filesystem's block mapping, using an approach that is distinct from raw partitions, swap file I/O *does* issue direct device I/O. The kernel uses the filesystem's block mapping (built in `swapon`) to build a `swap_extent` tree, and then uses this structure (e.g., via `bio` I/O operations) to send I/O directly to the block device. It is critical to note that the kernel does *not* go through the filesystem's standard I/O path. This is a critical point that needs correction. Only specialized filesystems (e.g., NFS with `SWP_FS_OPS` set) fall back to filesystem's `a_ops->swap_rw` implementation, but this is the exception, not the rule.

4.  **Write Coalescing Description:** The current wording regarding write coalescing under writeback is technically inaccurate. The documentation claims that "if a page is modified and swapped out again before the previous write completes, the swap cache absorbs the update without issuing a new write." This statement is incorrect. If a page is modified *while* a previous write is in progress (`PG_writeback` is set), the initial write will continue with the old data.  The page becomes dirty again (`PG_dirty`). Because the first write has already been issued with the old data, the kernel *must* issue a new write to persist the modifications *after* the initial write completes. It cannot skip issuing the write. While coalescing is possible if the dirtying happens *before* a write, this is not the case here.

3. Because the old write has already been issued with the old data, the kernel *must* issue a new write operation to persist the new modifications after the current writeback completes. It cannot 'absorb' the update to cancel the need for a new write.

Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Swap

This commit fills in the swap.rst stub as part of the structured memory
management documentation following Mel Gorman's book outline.

> +Cluster Allocation
> +------------------
> +
> +Swap space is allocated in clusters (groups of contiguous slots, typically
> +32 pages).  Each cluster tracks which slots are free and whether it has

Is the cluster size typically 32 pages?

The macro for swap disk allocation clusters, SWAPFILE_CLUSTER, is usually 256
(or HPAGE_PMD_NR, typically 512). The value 32 appears to correspond to
SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX, which is a distinct concept used as a batch size limit for
page reclaim.

> +pending discards.  Per-CPU hints point to the most recently used cluster
> +so that allocations from the same CPU tend to land in the same cluster,
[ ... ]
> +Swap Cache
> +==========
> +
> +The swap cache keeps recently swapped-in (or about to be swapped-out)
> +pages in memory, indexed by their swap entry.  This serves several
> +purposes:
> +
> +- **Deduplication**: when multiple processes share a swapped page (via
> +  ``fork()``), only one copy is read from disk; subsequent faults find
> +  the page in the swap cache.
> +- **Write coalescing**: if a page is modified and swapped out again before
> +  the previous write completes, the swap cache absorbs the update without
> +  issuing a new write.

Does the swap cache actually absorb the update without issuing a new write?

If a page is modified while a previous write is in-flight (PG_writeback is
set), the old data is already being written to disk. The page becomes dirty
again (PG_dirty), so the kernel must issue a new write operation to persist
the new modifications after the current writeback completes.

> +- **Readahead**: when one page is swapped in, adjacent swap entries are
> +  speculatively read to exploit spatial and temporal locality.
[ ... ]
> +Writeback
> +---------
> +
> +zswap writeback decompresses the page, allocates a swap slot, and writes
> +the uncompressed page to the swap device.  This is the slow path —

Does zswap writeback allocate a new swap slot?

The swap slot (swp_entry_t) is already allocated by the core swap code
(folio_alloc_swap()) before the page is ever passed to zswap_store().
During writeback, zswap_writeback_entry() uses this pre-existing swap entry
to allocate a swap cache folio and write to the already-allocated disk slot.

> +ideally most pages are either faulted back in from the compressed cache
> +or freed without ever reaching disk.
[ ... ]
> +Swap I/O
> +========
> +
> +``mm/page_io.c`` handles the mechanics of reading and writing pages to
> +swap.  The I/O path checks three layers in order before falling through to
> +disk:
> +
> +1. The zero page bitmap — if the slot is known to be zero-filled, return
> +   a zeroed page (read) or set the bit (write) with no I/O.
> +2. zswap — if enabled, attempt to store/load the page in the compressed
> +   cache.
> +3. Block I/O — submit a bio to the swap device, using the swap extent
> +   tree to map the slot to a disk sector.
> +
> +For swap files (as opposed to raw partitions), the I/O follows the
> +filesystem's block mapping rather than issuing direct device I/O.

Do swap files avoid direct device I/O?

Standard swap I/O operations (like swap_writepage_bdev_async) use the
swap_extent tree built during swapon to submit raw bios directly to the
underlying block device. This bypasses the filesystem's normal I/O routines.
Only specialized filesystems (like NFS with SWP_FS_OPS set) fall back to
the filesystem's a_ops->swap_rw.
